subject: civil_litigation
fact_pattern: Mensah Corporate Law LLP is advising Osei Manufacturing Inc. on a regulatory compliance matter. Osei's general counsel asks the litigation partner at Mensah for a written legal opinion on whether Osei's current waste disposal practices comply with the Environmental Protection Act. The opinion is marked confidential. Osei is later sued by a neighbouring landowner alleging environmental contamination, and the plaintiff demands production of the legal opinion in the affidavit of documents.
call_of_question: Is the Mensah Legal opinion protected by solicitor-client privilege, and must Osei disclose it in its affidavit of documents?

options.A: No — the opinion relates to regulatory compliance, not litigation, so it is only protected by litigation privilege and must be produced once the litigation ends.
options.B: Yes — the opinion is protected by solicitor-client privilege because it is a confidential communication between lawyer and client made for the purpose of obtaining legal advice; it must be listed in Schedule B of Osei's affidavit of documents but need not be produced.
options.C: No — solicitor-client privilege applies only to communications made during the course of litigation, so pre-litigation legal opinions are not protected.
options.D: Yes — the opinion is protected, and Osei is not required to even acknowledge that the opinion exists in its affidavit of documents.

explanation: Under the Rules of Civil Procedure, r. 30.03(2), documents for which privilege is claimed must be listed in Schedule B of the affidavit of documents — the privilege claim must be made, but the document need not be produced. Solicitor-client privilege attaches to a confidential communication between a lawyer and client made for the purpose of giving or obtaining legal advice. The three elements are satisfied here: (1) the communication is between Mensah (lawyer) and Osei (client); (2) it involves the giving of legal advice on regulatory compliance; and (3) it is marked confidential and intended to be so. Solicitor-client privilege is permanent — it does not expire when litigation ends — and it applies to pre-litigation legal advice, not only to communications made during litigation.
why_A_wrong: Solicitor-client privilege applies to legal advice communications regardless of whether they arise in the context of litigation. Litigation privilege is the narrower doctrine that applies to documents prepared for litigation and expires when litigation ends. This opinion satisfies solicitor-client privilege, which is permanent.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. The opinion satisfies all three elements of solicitor-client privilege and must be listed in Schedule B of the affidavit of documents under r. 30.03(2), but need not be produced.
why_C_wrong: Solicitor-client privilege is not limited to communications made during litigation. It applies to all confidential lawyer-client communications made for the purpose of obtaining legal advice, including pre-litigation regulatory opinions.
why_D_wrong: Rule 30.03(2) requires that privileged documents be listed in Schedule B of the affidavit of documents. The existence of the document must be disclosed; only the content is protected by the privilege claim.

source_reference.subject: Civil Litigation
source_reference.heading: Solicitor-Client Privilege in Discovery
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r. 30.03(2)

subject: civil_litigation
fact_pattern: Delorme Logistics Inc. is a defendant in a commercial fraud action. During preparation of the affidavit of documents, counsel identifies two categories of documents: (A) emails between Delorme's CEO and outside counsel discussing the CEO's litigation strategy and legal exposure — created after the claim was issued; and (B) a legal memorandum prepared by outside counsel advising Delorme on the legality of its pricing practices, written eight months before any litigation was contemplated.
call_of_question: Which statement correctly describes the privilege status of each category of documents?

options.A: Category A is protected by litigation privilege only; Category B is not protected by any privilege because it predates the litigation.
options.B: Category A is protected by both solicitor-client privilege and litigation privilege; Category B is protected by solicitor-client privilege only. Both must be listed in Schedule B but need not be produced.
options.C: Category A is protected by litigation privilege only, which will expire when the litigation ends; Category B is not privileged at all.
options.D: Neither category is privileged because Delorme is a corporation and the privilege belongs to the individual officers, not the entity.

explanation: Under settled Ontario privilege law, Category A — litigation strategy communications between the CEO and outside counsel after the claim was issued — satisfies both solicitor-client privilege (confidential lawyer-client communications for legal advice) and litigation privilege (documents prepared for the dominant purpose of litigation, per Blank v Canada, 2006 SCC 39). Category B — the pre-litigation legal memorandum on pricing legality — satisfies solicitor-client privilege because it is a confidential communication between lawyer and client for the purpose of obtaining legal advice; this privilege is permanent and applies regardless of whether litigation was anticipated. Litigation privilege does not apply to Category B because litigation was not anticipated when it was created. Both documents must be listed in Schedule B of the affidavit of documents under r. 30.03(2).
why_A_wrong: Category A satisfies solicitor-client privilege as well as litigation privilege. Category B is protected by solicitor-client privilege — the privilege applies to pre-litigation legal advice and does not require that litigation be anticipated.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Category A is protected by both privileges; Category B by solicitor-client privilege only. Both must be listed in Schedule B under r. 30.03(2).
why_C_wrong: Litigation privilege for Category A does not expire simply because it is identified during litigation. More importantly, solicitor-client privilege for Category B is permanent and pre-litigation advice is fully protected.
why_D_wrong: Corporations hold solicitor-client privilege as clients. The privilege does not belong only to individual officers; it belongs to the corporate client and is exercised by authorised representatives.

source_reference.subject: Civil Litigation
source_reference.heading: Solicitor-Client Privilege in Discovery
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r. 30.03(2); Blank v Canada, 2006 SCC 39

subject: civil_litigation
fact_pattern: Przybylski Energy Corp. is involved in a commercial dispute with Nadarajah Pipeline Ltd. During examination for discovery, Przybylski's president voluntarily discloses the general conclusions of a privileged legal opinion — specifically stating that 'counsel's opinion confirmed our contract interpretation was correct' — in order to explain the company's decision-making. Nadarajah subsequently seeks full production of the privileged opinion, arguing that privilege has been waived.
call_of_question: Has Przybylski waived solicitor-client privilege over the full legal opinion by disclosing its general conclusions during the examination for discovery?

options.A: No — a party may reveal the conclusions of a privileged opinion without waiving privilege over the document itself; only production of the full document constitutes waiver.
options.B: No — privilege is waived only if the disclosure was made in writing and signed by the party's legal counsel.
options.C: Yes — by voluntarily disclosing the substance or conclusions of a privileged communication in the course of litigation, Przybylski has impliedly waived privilege over the opinion, entitling Nadarajah to production of the full document.
options.D: Yes — but only with respect to the single sentence disclosed; the remainder of the opinion retains privilege.

explanation: Under Ontario privilege law, solicitor-client privilege may be waived expressly or impliedly. Implied waiver occurs where a party voluntarily discloses the substance or conclusions of a privileged communication in a manner inconsistent with maintaining the confidentiality of the full document. By stating that counsel's opinion confirmed the company's contract interpretation, Przybylski has disclosed the advice in a way that makes it unfair to shield the underlying opinion from scrutiny. Courts have consistently held that partial disclosure of the substance of a privileged communication in the course of proceedings constitutes implied waiver that extends to the full communication, preventing selective use of privilege as both a shield and a sword.
why_A_wrong: Revealing the substance or conclusions of a privileged communication — not merely the existence of the document — can constitute implied waiver. The waiver is not limited to producing the physical document; it occurs through disclosure of the privileged content.
why_B_wrong: Waiver of privilege does not require a written signed disclosure by counsel. Implied waiver arises from conduct — including oral disclosure during discovery — that is inconsistent with maintaining confidentiality.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Voluntary disclosure of the substance or conclusions of a privileged communication constitutes implied waiver, entitling the opposing party to production of the full document.
why_D_wrong: Where implied waiver occurs through disclosure of the substance of a privileged communication, the waiver typically extends to the entirety of the relevant communication, not merely the words disclosed. Selective privilege — using it as a shield while disclosing favourable portions — is not permitted.

source_reference.subject: Civil Litigation
source_reference.heading: Solicitor-Client Privilege in Discovery
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r. 30.03(2)

subject: civil_litigation
fact_pattern: Castellano Pharma Inc. employs an in-house legal counsel, Dr. Osei-Bonsu, who holds a law degree and is a member of the Law Society of Ontario. In practice, Dr. Osei-Bonsu divides his time equally between providing legal advice to the executive team and acting as a business strategist on product pricing and commercial negotiations. During document production in a class action, the plaintiff seeks production of emails between the CEO and Dr. Osei-Bonsu discussing pricing strategy decisions in which Dr. Osei-Bonsu occasionally added brief references to regulatory risk.
call_of_question: Are the emails between the CEO and Dr. Osei-Bonsu protected by solicitor-client privilege?

options.A: Yes — all communications between a client and an individual who is a member of the Law Society of Ontario are automatically protected by solicitor-client privilege.
options.B: No — in-house counsel can never give rise to solicitor-client privilege because they are employees of the client, not independent legal advisors.
options.C: It depends — solicitor-client privilege applies only to those portions of the emails where Dr. Osei-Bonsu was acting in his legal capacity giving legal advice; communications where he was acting as a business strategist on pricing and commercial matters are not protected.
options.D: Yes — any email that contains even a single reference to regulatory risk qualifies as a communication for the purpose of legal advice, protecting the entire email.

explanation: Under the law confirmed in Pritchard v Ontario (Human Rights Commission), 2004 SCC 31 as applied to in-house counsel, solicitor-client privilege applies to communications with in-house counsel only where the lawyer was acting in their legal capacity — giving or seeking legal advice — and the communication was intended to be confidential. Where in-house counsel acts in a business, strategic, or commercial capacity, the communications are not privileged. Courts undertake a document-by-document or communication-by-communication analysis to separate legal advice components from business advice components. The emails where Dr. Osei-Bonsu acted as a business strategist on pricing are not privileged; those where he provided legal advice on regulatory risk may be privileged. A brief regulatory reference embedded in a predominantly business communication does not automatically make the full email privileged.
why_A_wrong: LSO membership does not automatically confer privilege on all communications. The privilege requires the lawyer to be acting in their legal capacity giving legal advice, and the communication to be confidential. In-house counsel acting in a business role are not covered.
why_B_wrong: In-house counsel can give rise to solicitor-client privilege. Employment by the client does not negate the privilege. What matters is whether the lawyer was acting in a legal capacity, not independence per se.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Privilege applies only where Dr. Osei-Bonsu was acting in his legal advisory capacity. Communications where he functioned as a business strategist are not privileged, even if he happens to hold a law degree.
why_D_wrong: A single passing reference to regulatory risk does not transform a predominantly business communication into a privileged legal advice document. Courts look at the dominant character and purpose of the communication as a whole.

source_reference.subject: Civil Litigation
source_reference.heading: Solicitor-Client Privilege in Discovery
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r. 30.03(2); Pritchard v Ontario (Human Rights Commission), 2004 SCC 31

tested_concepts: in-house counsel privilege — legal vs business capacity
Pritchard v Ontario (Human Rights Commission), 2004 SCC 31

subject: civil_litigation
fact_pattern: Giordano Wealth Management Corp. successfully defended a securities misrepresentation lawsuit brought by Tremblay Investment Fund LP three years ago. The action was dismissed after a full trial, and the matter has been concluded. Giordano now faces a new claim by a different plaintiff, Nakamura Pension Trust, arising from the same alleged misrepresentation. Nakamura seeks production of legal memoranda that Giordano's outside counsel prepared in connection with the now-concluded Tremblay litigation, arguing that solicitor-client privilege and litigation privilege both expired when the Tremblay action ended.

options.A: Yes — both solicitor-client privilege and litigation privilege expire when the litigation to which they relate concludes; all documents from the Tremblay action must now be produced.
options.B: No — both solicitor-client privilege and litigation privilege are permanent and survive the conclusion of all related proceedings.
options.C: Partly — litigation privilege over the memoranda expired when the Tremblay action concluded, but solicitor-client privilege remains intact and continues to protect those memoranda in the new Nakamura proceeding.
options.D: Partly — solicitor-client privilege expired with the Tremblay action because it was created in a litigation context, but litigation privilege survives and continues to protect the memoranda.

explanation: Under Ontario privilege law, solicitor-client privilege and litigation privilege are distinct doctrines with different durations. Solicitor-client privilege is permanent — it survives the conclusion of the retainer, the litigation, and the client relationship. It cannot be eroded by the passage of time or the end of proceedings. Litigation privilege, by contrast, is temporary — as confirmed in Blank v Canada, 2006 SCC 39, it exists to protect the adversarial process and expires when the litigation for which the documents were created has concluded. Accordingly, litigation privilege over the Tremblay memoranda ended when that action was dismissed, but solicitor-client privilege over any portions that constitute confidential legal advice communications remains fully intact and protects those documents from production in the Nakamura proceeding.
why_A_wrong: Solicitor-client privilege does not expire when litigation ends. It is permanent. Only litigation privilege expires at the end of proceedings. Nakamura's argument is incorrect as to solicitor-client privilege.
why_B_wrong: Litigation privilege is not permanent — it expires when the litigation ends, as confirmed in Blank v Canada, 2006 SCC 39. Only solicitor-client privilege survives indefinitely.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Litigation privilege expired when the Tremblay action concluded, but solicitor-client privilege remains intact and continues to protect the legal advice communications in the Nakamura proceeding.
why_D_wrong: This reverses the correct rule. Solicitor-client privilege is the permanent doctrine; litigation privilege is the temporary one. Creating documents in a litigation context does not convert solicitor-client privilege into a temporary doctrine.

source_reference.subject: Civil Litigation
source_reference.heading: Solicitor-Client Privilege in Discovery
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r. 30.03(2); Blank v Canada, 2006 SCC 39

tested_concepts: litigation privilege expires when litigation ends

subject: civil_litigation
fact_pattern: Beaumont Construction Ltd. is defending a claim for defective workmanship. After the statement of claim was issued, Beaumont's project manager retained a structural engineering firm to inspect the building and prepare a report. The engineer prepared the report at the request of Beaumont's litigation counsel. The plaintiff now demands production of the engineering report on discovery, arguing it is not privileged because it was prepared by a third party, not by counsel.
call_of_question: Is the engineering report protected by litigation privilege?

options.A: No — litigation privilege applies only to documents created by or exchanged directly between the lawyer and client; third-party reports are never protected.
options.B: Yes — any document created after litigation commences is automatically protected by litigation privilege.
options.C: Yes — provided that the dominant purpose for which the report was created was for use in the existing litigation; litigation privilege extends to communications with third parties when this dominant purpose test is met.
options.D: No — only solicitor-client privilege can protect third-party reports, and it would not apply here because the engineer is not Beaumont's lawyer.

explanation: The Supreme Court of Canada in Blank v. Canada (Minister of Justice), 2006 SCC 39 confirmed that litigation privilege protects documents and communications — including those with third parties — whose dominant purpose is preparation for the conduct of reasonably anticipated or existing litigation. The privilege is not limited to lawyer-client communications; it extends to communications between a party (or their counsel) and third parties, such as expert witnesses, investigators, or consultants, so long as the dominant purpose of the communication is litigation preparation. Here, the structural engineering report was prepared at counsel's request specifically for the purpose of defending the existing litigation; the dominant purpose test is satisfied.
why_A_wrong: Litigation privilege is broader than solicitor-client privilege and explicitly extends to communications with third parties, provided the dominant purpose of the document is preparation for litigation. It is not restricted to direct lawyer-client communications.
why_B_wrong: Not every document created after litigation commences is automatically protected. The dominant purpose of the document's creation must be the conduct of the litigation. Documents created for other purposes after the claim was issued are not privileged.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Litigation privilege extends to third-party communications and documents when the dominant purpose of their creation is the existing or anticipated litigation, per Blank v. Canada, 2006 SCC 39.
why_D_wrong: Solicitor-client privilege is not the only protection available for third-party documents. Litigation privilege specifically applies to third-party communications prepared for the dominant purpose of litigation.

source_reference.subject: Civil Litigation
source_reference.heading: Litigation Privilege — Dominant Purpose Test
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Blank v. Canada (Minister of Justice), 2006 SCC 39; Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.30.03(2)

tested_concepts: third-party communications covered by litigation privilege

subject: civil_litigation
fact_pattern: Vandenberg Shipping Inc. settled a cargo damage claim brought by Okafor Importers Ltd. in 2021. The settlement was reached before trial and the action was dismissed. A separate action is now brought by a third party, Leblanc Freight Ltd., alleging it was damaged by the same cargo handling incident. Leblanc seeks production of investigation reports and witness statements that Vandenberg's counsel prepared in the 2021 Okafor action, arguing they are relevant to the current proceeding.
call_of_question: Are Vandenberg's investigation reports and witness statements from the concluded Okafor action protected by litigation privilege in the current Leblanc proceeding?

options.A: Yes — litigation privilege is permanent, like solicitor-client privilege, and the documents remain protected in all future proceedings.
options.B: No — litigation privilege expired when the Okafor action was settled and dismissed; the documents are no longer protected and must be produced in the Leblanc action.
options.C: Yes — litigation privilege continues to apply to the documents in the new proceeding because the new claim arises from the same incident.
options.D: No — investigation reports prepared by counsel are never protected by litigation privilege; only direct communications between lawyer and client are covered.

explanation: As confirmed by the Supreme Court of Canada in Blank v. Canada (Minister of Justice), 2006 SCC 39, litigation privilege is temporary and expires when the litigation for which the documents were prepared has concluded — whether by settlement, dismissal, judgment, or otherwise. Unlike solicitor-client privilege, which is permanent, litigation privilege exists solely to protect the adversarial process in the specific litigation for which the documents were created. Once the Okafor action was settled and dismissed, litigation privilege over the investigation reports and witness statements expired. Those documents are no longer protected in the subsequent Leblanc action (though solicitor-client privilege may independently protect any portions that constitute legal advice communications).
why_A_wrong: Litigation privilege is not permanent. The Supreme Court in Blank v. Canada expressly distinguished it from solicitor-client privilege and confirmed it expires when the litigation ends.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Litigation privilege expired when the Okafor action was settled and dismissed, so the documents are no longer protected in the Leblanc proceeding.
why_C_wrong: The fact that the new claim arises from the same incident does not extend the litigation privilege from the concluded Okafor action to the new Leblanc proceeding. Litigation privilege attaches to the specific proceeding, not to the underlying incident.
why_D_wrong: Investigation reports and witness statements prepared for the dominant purpose of litigation are protected by litigation privilege. The privilege is not limited to direct lawyer-client communications.

source_reference.subject: Civil Litigation
source_reference.heading: Litigation Privilege — Duration
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Blank v. Canada (Minister of Justice), 2006 SCC 39; Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.30.03(2)

tested_concepts: litigation privilege is temporary — expires when litigation ends

subject: civil_litigation
subtopic_heading: Implied Undertaking Rule — Collateral Use Prohibited
fact_pattern: Novak Pharmaceuticals Inc. is a defendant in a product liability action brought by several plaintiffs. During documentary discovery, Novak produces internal safety test results to the plaintiffs under compulsion of the Rules of Civil Procedure. One of the plaintiffs, without obtaining a court order or Novak's consent, provides copies of the safety test results to a journalist, who publishes them in an investigative article. Novak brings a motion alleging a breach of the implied undertaking rule.
call_of_question: Which of the following best describes the implied undertaking rule and its application to these facts?

options.A: The implied undertaking rule only applies to information obtained on oral examination for discovery, not to documents produced in the affidavit of documents process.
options.B: The implied undertaking rule under r.30.1.01 of the Rules of Civil Procedure prohibits a party who receives documents through compelled disclosure from using those documents for any purpose other than the litigation in which they were produced; providing them to a journalist for publication breaches this rule.
options.C: The implied undertaking rule applies only to privileged documents; since Novak produced the safety results as non-privileged documents, there is no implied undertaking over them.
options.D: The implied undertaking rule prohibits use of documents outside the litigation unless the opposing party consents, but publication by a journalist is a protected exercise of freedom of expression that overrides the undertaking.

explanation: Rule 30.1.01 of the Rules of Civil Procedure codifies the implied undertaking rule in Ontario. It provides that all documents and information obtained through the discovery process — including documents produced in the affidavit of documents under compulsion of the Rules — are subject to an implied undertaking that they will be used only for the purposes of the litigation in which they were produced and will not be used for any other collateral purpose. The rule applies to all compelled disclosure, not only privileged documents or examination for discovery transcripts. Providing the safety test results to a journalist for publication in an article is plainly a collateral use outside the litigation and constitutes a breach of the implied undertaking under r.30.1.01. The undertaking can be released by court order or with the consent of the producing party, neither of which was obtained here.
why_A_wrong: The implied undertaking rule under r.30.1.01 applies to all documents and information obtained through the discovery process, including documents produced in the affidavit of documents. It is not limited to oral examination for discovery.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Rule 30.1.01 imposes an implied undertaking against collateral use of all compelled disclosure, and providing the documents to a journalist for publication is a clear breach.
why_C_wrong: The implied undertaking rule applies to all compelled disclosure — privileged and non-privileged alike. The fact that the documents were not privileged does not exempt them from the implied undertaking against collateral use.
why_D_wrong: Freedom of expression does not override the implied undertaking rule in the context of compelled civil discovery. The undertaking is a court-imposed obligation; it can be relieved only by court order or consent of the producing party, not by reliance on a constitutional freedom.

source_reference.subject: Civil Litigation
source_reference.heading: Implied Undertaking Rule — Collateral Use Prohibited
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.30.1.01

tested_concepts: prohibition on collateral use of discovered documents
exceptions to implied undertaking — court order or consent
